title: Filtering Groups (HAVING)
description: Learn the difference between WHERE and HAVING.

slides.title: The WHERE Limitation 🛑
slides.content:
# I only want the big groups!

You just created a query showing Total Sales Per Country using `GROUP BY`.

Now, you only want to see the countries whose Total Sales are *greater than $10,000*. 

Instinctively, you try to use `WHERE SUM(sales) > 10000`. 

**CRASH! ERROR! ERROR!**

slides.title: The Bouncer Timeline ⏱️
slides.content:
## Why did WHERE fail?

The `WHERE` bouncer always checks rows *before* they get grouped and summed up. He literally runs before the `SUM()` math happens, so he has no clue what the final sum is!

To filter *after* the math happens, we need a new bouncer: **`HAVING`**.

quizData.question: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?

options.text: They are exactly the same, HAVING is just the updated 2023 version
WHERE filters raw individual rows BEFORE grouping. HAVING filters the aggregated math totals AFTER grouping.
HAVING filters letters, WHERE filters numbers

quizData.explanation: WHERE evaluates each raw row. HAVING evaluates the new mathematical 'group' (e.g. enforcing that a group's COUNT > 50).

slides.title: Using HAVING 🛡️
slides.content:
## The Post-Group Filter

The `HAVING` clause goes immediately *after* the `GROUP BY` clause.

`SELECT country, SUM(sales) FROM orders GROUP BY country HAVING SUM(sales) > 10000;`

The database groups everything, performs the math, looks at the results, and discards whichever country fell short.

quizData.question: What is the correct structural order of clauses in an advanced SQL query?

options.text: SELECT > FROM > GROUP BY > WHERE > HAVING
SELECT > FROM > WHERE > GROUP BY > HAVING
SELECT > FROM > HAVING > WHERE > GROUP BY

quizData.explanation: First retrieve (SELECT/FROM). Second, filter raw rows (WHERE). Third, smash them together (GROUP BY). Fourth, filter the smashed results (HAVING).

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Group the users by `country`, Count them via `COUNT(*)`, and only show groups that HAVE a count `>` 5.
slides.codeSnippet:
SELECT country, COUNT(*)
FROM users
GROUP BY country
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
